sections.heading: What are ViewGroups?
sections.content: A ViewGroup is a special type of View that acts as a container for other Views and ViewGroups. It is responsible for measuring, positioning, and sizing its child Views according to its own layout rules. Choosing the right ViewGroup for your UI is critical — the wrong choice leads to deeply nested hierarchies that hurt performance, while the right choice produces flat, efficient layouts. Android provides several built-in ViewGroups, the most important being ConstraintLayout and LinearLayout.

rows.description: Positions children using constraints between views and parent edges — flat hierarchy
rows.best_for: Complex, responsive screens — recommended for most layouts

rows.description: Arranges children in a single row (horizontal) or column (vertical)
Positions children relative to each other or the parent edges
rows.best_for: Legacy — replaced by ConstraintLayout in modern apps

rows.description: Stacks children on top of each other — last child is drawn on top
rows.best_for: Fragment containers, overlapping Views, loading overlays

rows.description: Arranges children in a grid of rows and columns
rows.best_for: Simple grid arrangements without dynamic data

rows.description: Advanced FrameLayout that coordinates animations between child Views
rows.best_for: AppBar collapsing, Snackbar behavior, FAB interactions

rows.description: Subclass of ConstraintLayout with built-in animation between states

sections.heading: LinearLayout
sections.content: LinearLayout is the simplest and most intuitive ViewGroup. It arranges its child Views in a single direction — either vertically (one below another) or horizontally (one beside another). Its key strength is simplicity: easy to understand, easy to use, and perfect for forms, toolbars, or any stack of items. Its weakness is that nesting multiple LinearLayouts creates deep view hierarchies that slow down rendering.

sections.heading: LinearLayout Key Attributes
items: android:orientation – Required attribute: vertical (stack top to bottom) or horizontal (side by side)
android:gravity – Aligns all children within the LinearLayout (e.g., center, end, bottom)
android:layout_gravity – Aligns an individual child within its parent LinearLayout
android:layout_weight – Distributes remaining space proportionally among children
android:weightSum – Defines the total weight sum (optional; defaults to sum of all child weights)
android:divider – Adds a drawable divider between children
android:showDividers – Controls where dividers appear: beginning, middle, end, or none
android:baselineAligned – Aligns children by their text baseline in horizontal layouts

rows.scenario: Two equal-width buttons side by side
rows.result: Each button takes exactly 50% of the available width

rows.scenario: One button takes twice the space
rows.setup: Button A: weight=2, Button B: weight=1 (layout_width=0dp)
rows.result: Button A gets 2/3 width, Button B gets 1/3 width

rows.scenario: EditText fills remaining space beside a Button
rows.result: Button takes its natural size, EditText fills the rest

rows.scenario: Three equal-height rows in vertical LinearLayout
rows.result: Each View takes exactly 1/3 of the available height

sections.heading: ConstraintLayout
sections.content: ConstraintLayout is the most powerful and recommended layout for Android development. It allows you to create complex, responsive UIs with a completely flat view hierarchy — no nesting required. Each child View is positioned by defining constraints that connect it to the parent edges, other Views, or guidelines. The Layout Editor in Android Studio provides a visual drag-and-drop interface for building ConstraintLayouts without writing XML manually.

sections.heading: Why ConstraintLayout is Preferred
items: Flat view hierarchy — all Views are direct children, no nested layouts needed
Better performance — single measurement pass instead of recursive nested passes
Responsive — Views adapt to different screen sizes using constraints and guidelines
Powerful positioning — align to parent, other views, or virtual helpers like Guidelines and Barriers
Built-in chains — distribute multiple Views evenly like a flex layout
Supports aspect ratio constraints — lock a View to a specific width:height ratio
Fully supported in the Android Studio Layout Editor with visual handles
Supports MotionLayout for advanced animations as a direct upgrade path

rows.description: Aligns the top edge of this View to the top edge of another View or parent
Aligns the bottom edge of this View to the bottom edge of another View or parent
Aligns the start (left) edge of this View to the start edge of another View or parent
Aligns the end (right) edge of this View to the end edge of another View or parent
Places the top of this View below the bottom of another View
Places the bottom of this View above the top of another View
Places the start of this View to the right of another View's end
Places the end of this View to the left of another View's start

sections.heading: ConstraintLayout — Centering & Bias
items: To center horizontally: constrain both Start and End to parent — View centers automatically
To center vertically: constrain both Top and Bottom to parent — View centers automatically
app:layout_constraintHorizontal_bias – Shifts position between 0.0 (start) and 1.0 (end); default 0.5 (center)
app:layout_constraintVertical_bias – Shifts position between 0.0 (top) and 1.0 (bottom); default 0.5
Bias of 0.3 means the View sits 30% from the start/top — useful for off-center layouts
Bias only works when the View has constraints on both opposite sides
To center a View exactly: set both constraints to parent and leave bias at default 0.5

rows.behavior: View sizes itself to fit its content; respects constraints for positioning but not size
Avoid in ConstraintLayout — use 0dp (match constraint) instead
View expands to fill the space defined by its constraints — the ConstraintLayout equivalent of match_parent
Exact fixed size regardless of constraints

sections.heading: Guidelines in ConstraintLayout
sections.content: Guidelines are invisible helper lines you can add to a ConstraintLayout to create consistent alignment points. They can be positioned as a fixed dp offset from an edge or as a percentage of the layout's width/height. Multiple Views can be constrained to the same Guideline, making it easy to align a column of Views or create a two-column layout without nesting.

sections.heading: Guidelines, Barriers & Groups
items: Guideline – An invisible vertical or horizontal line used as an alignment anchor for other Views
app:layout_constraintGuide_percent – Positions the Guideline at a percentage of the parent (e.g., 0.5 = center)
app:layout_constraintGuide_begin – Positions the Guideline at a fixed dp offset from the start/top
app:layout_constraintGuide_end – Positions the Guideline at a fixed dp offset from the end/bottom
Barrier – A dynamic Guideline that positions itself based on the size of multiple referenced Views
app:barrierDirection – Direction the Barrier extends: start, end, top, or bottom
app:constraint_referenced_ids – Comma-separated list of View IDs the Barrier tracks
Group – A virtual helper that controls the visibility of multiple Views at once with a single visibility change

rows.behavior: Views are spread evenly across the available space including edges
rows.use_case: Evenly distributed Views with space between them and the edges

rows.behavior: Views are spread evenly but the first and last Views are attached to the chain edges
rows.use_case: Evenly distributed Views with no space at the edges

rows.behavior: Views are packed together in the center of the chain
rows.use_case: Grouping Views together and centering the group

rows.behavior: Views are packed together and shifted by horizontal or vertical bias

sections.heading: Creating a Chain in ConstraintLayout
items: A chain is created when two or more Views are connected to each other bidirectionally
View A's End is constrained to View B's Start, AND View B's Start is constrained to View A's End
The first View in the chain (chain head) controls the chain style with app:layout_constraintHorizontal_chainStyle
Set layout_width=0dp on chain members to distribute available space proportionally
Use layout_constraintHorizontal_weight on each View to control its share of the space
Chains work in both horizontal and vertical directions
In Android Studio: select multiple Views → right-click → Chains → Create Horizontal/Vertical Chain

sections.heading: ConstraintLayout vs LinearLayout — When to Use Which

rows.scenario: Complex screen with many views at different positions
rows.reason: Flat hierarchy handles any position without nesting

rows.scenario: Simple vertical form with equally-spaced fields
rows.reason: Simpler XML, easier to read and maintain

rows.scenario: Row of icons or buttons side by side
rows.reason: Both work; LinearLayout is simpler for uniform rows

rows.recommended: LinearLayout with weight=1 or ConstraintLayout chain
rows.reason: LinearLayout weight is simpler; ConstraintLayout avoids nesting

rows.scenario: RecyclerView item with icon, title, and subtitle
rows.reason: Efficient flat layout for repeated item rows

rows.scenario: Dialog with title, message, and two buttons
rows.reason: Simple stacked content — LinearLayout is ideal

sections.heading: FrameLayout
sections.content: FrameLayout is the simplest ViewGroup — it stacks its children directly on top of each other, with the last child drawn on top. It is most commonly used as a container for Fragments, where the system swaps Fragment views in and out of the FrameLayout. It is also used for overlapping UI elements like a loading spinner over content, or a badge over an icon.

sections.heading: FrameLayout Common Uses
items: Fragment container — the most common use: <FrameLayout android:id='@+id/fragment_container' />
Overlay a loading spinner (ProgressBar) on top of content
Display a badge or notification count overlapping an icon
Show/hide an error or empty state View on top of a list
android:layout_gravity on children controls where each child is placed within the FrameLayout
Use View.GONE and View.VISIBLE to toggle which child is shown at a time
Avoid putting multiple simultaneously visible, non-overlapping children in FrameLayout

rows.affects: Space inside the View between its border and its content
rows.example: paddingTop=16dp pushes the text 16dp away from the View's top edge

rows.affects: Space outside the View between it and neighboring Views or parent edge
rows.example: marginTop=16dp pushes the View 16dp away from the View above it

rows.affects: RTL-aware padding on the start or end side
rows.example: Use instead of paddingLeft/Right for proper RTL support

rows.affects: RTL-aware margin on the start or end side
rows.example: Use instead of marginLeft/Right for proper RTL support

sections.heading: Layout Performance Tips
items: Prefer ConstraintLayout to avoid nested LinearLayouts — each nesting level adds measurement overhead
Use the Layout Inspector in Android Studio to visualize and debug your view hierarchy at runtime
Lint warnings like 'Nested weights are bad for performance' should always be resolved
Replace nested LinearLayouts with a single ConstraintLayout for better rendering performance
Use <merge> as the root of included layouts to eliminate the extra ViewGroup wrapper
Use ViewStub for UI that is only sometimes visible — it inflates lazily on demand
Avoid setting unnecessary backgrounds on intermediate ViewGroups to reduce overdraw
Enable 'Show GPU Overdraw' in Developer Options to visualize and reduce overdraw in your layouts

rows.scope: Affects the content inside the View
rows.example: android:gravity="center" centers the text inside a TextView

rows.scope: Affects the View itself within its parent
rows.example: android:layout_gravity="end" aligns the View to the end of its LinearLayout or FrameLayout parent

sections.heading: Best Practices for Layouts
items: Use ConstraintLayout as the root for any screen with more than 2-3 Views
Keep view hierarchy depth to 5 levels or fewer for optimal performance
Always use @dimen/ references for margins and padding instead of hardcoded values
Use @string/ for all text and @color/ for all colors — never hardcode in XML
Set android:contentDescription on every ImageView for accessibility compliance
Use start/end instead of left/right attributes for proper RTL language support
Preview layouts in multiple screen sizes using the Layout Editor's device selector
Extract repeated layout patterns into separate layout files and use <include>

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: ConstraintLayout and LinearLayout are the two layouts you will use in the vast majority of Android screens. ConstraintLayout excels at complex, flat, responsive layouts with its powerful constraint, chain, guideline, and barrier system. LinearLayout shines in its simplicity for straightforward stacked or side-by-side arrangements. Understanding when and how to use each — along with FrameLayout for overlays and fragment containers — gives you the tools to build any UI efficiently.
